title: Why 1 Million Views Won't Pay Your Marketing Bills
excerpt: A wellness brand got 1.2M views from a fitness influencer. Zero sales. Here's the attribution problem killing Indian D2C brands and how to fix it.
content:
Let's start with a real story. A wellness brand — protein supplements, solid product, genuine fans, partnered with one of India's top fitness creators. The reel got 1.2 million views. The story slide got 340,000 impressions. The brand's Instagram grew by 4,200 followers overnight.

Sales from that campaign? Zero.

Not low. Not disappointing. Zero attributed sales. The influencer's audience was 78% male, 18–22, primarily from Tier 2 cities. The brand sold women's wellness supplements, priced at ₹1,800 a bottle, targeting working women aged 28–38 in metros. It was beautiful content shown to entirely the wrong people.

The Vanity Metric Trap

Indian D2C brands have developed an almost religious obsession with reach and impressions. It makes sense these numbers are big, they feel significant, and agencies love presenting them in pitch decks. But reach without attribution is just noise with a price tag attached.

The real question isn't "how many people saw this?" It's "how many people bought because of this?"

These are shockingly different questions with shockingly different answers.

What Attribution Actually Means

Attribution is the practice of connecting a marketing action to a business outcome. Did this influencer's post cause sales? Did this publication article drive signups? Did this affiliate link convert?

Without attribution infrastructure, you're flying blind. You'll overpay for reach that doesn't convert and underpay for creators who actually drive purchase intent.

The mechanics matter. You need:

  • Unique redirect links per creator, so every click is tracked back to its source
  • Discount codes as a secondary attribution layer, since some users screenshot codes and buy later
  • Cookie windows that give credit to creators even when a customer browses, leaves, and buys three days later
  • City and demographic data on who's actually clicking, not just who follows the creator

The Audience Mismatch Problem

Follower demographics and engaged audience demographics are different. And both are different from purchasing audience demographics.

A creator - might have 500,000 followers. Their engaged audience people who like, comment, save - might skew younger than their total base. Their purchasing audience - people who actually click links and buy - might be a completely different cohort.

Without tracking this at the individual creator level, you're making decisions based on averages that describe no one.

What Fixing This Looks Like

The brands getting this right are running tight attribution setups. Each influencer gets a tracked link and a unique code. Every campaign has a clear hypothesis: this creator's audience overlaps with our buyer persona in this city bracket at this price point.

Results are reviewed not by views but by cost-per-sale and revenue-per-creator. Creators who drive real sales at acceptable cost get renewals and rate increases. Creators who deliver views with no conversions get gracefully offboarded.

It sounds obvious. Most brands still aren't doing it.

The gap between brands that track attribution properly and brands that don't is becoming a competitive moat. Attribution is no longer a nice-to-have infrastructure investment. It's the difference between spending ₹5 lakh on marketing that compounds and spending ₹5 lakh on marketing that evaporates.

A million views is not a million rupees. But with the right attribution setup, even 50,000 targeted views from the right creator can drive revenue that justifies the spend ten times over.
author: MicroKorant Team

title: The Multi-Agency Problem Nobody Talks About
excerpt: Most Indian consumer brands work with 3–4 agencies simultaneously. The data fragmentation this creates costs brands lakhs every quarter — here's how to solve it.
content:
If you run marketing for a consumer brand in India, you probably have a situation that looks something like this: an influencer marketing agency managing your creator relationships, a PR and content studio handling publications and editorial placements, an affiliate network or manager running commission partnerships, and maybe a paid media agency running your Google and Meta campaigns.

That's four agencies, four reporting formats, four different definitions of what a "conversion" means, and four separate Excel sheets arriving in your inbox every Monday morning.

The Data Silo Problem

Each agency is incentivized to make their channel look good. The influencer agency reports reach, engagement, and brand mentions. The PR studio reports article placements, domain authority, and traffic estimates. The affiliate manager reports clicks, conversions, and commissions paid.

None of these numbers talk to each other. None of them share a common attribution layer. And the person who's supposed to synthesize this into a coherent marketing picture - usually a CMO or brand manager - is doing it in a spreadsheet on a Sunday evening.

This isn't a people problem. It's a systems problem.

What Gets Lost in Translation

When data lives in silos, you lose the ability to answer basic questions that should be easy:

  • Which channel drove the most revenue this month?
  • Is my influencer spend or my publication spend converting better?
  • Are the same customers converting across multiple channels, or are we reaching distinct audiences?
  • If I had ₹1 lakh more to spend, where should it go?

These aren't advanced analytics questions. They're foundational. But without unified attribution data, they're genuinely unanswerable.

The Agency Conflict Problem

There's another issue that doesn't get discussed: agencies aren't entirely objective about their own performance.

When your influencer agency reports numbers, they're naturally going to frame them in the most favorable light. When your publication studio reports CPC, they're going to use the methodology that makes their numbers look best. This isn't malicious — it's human nature. Every vendor presents their best case.

As a client, you don't have the infrastructure to independently verify what each agency is telling you. You're trusting their spreadsheets.

A Better Architecture

The solution isn't to distrust your agencies. They're doing good work. The solution is to own your attribution layer independently of any agency.

When every agency is plugging into the same tracking infrastructure the same redirect links, the same attribution window, the same cookie logic their numbers become comparable. You can finally answer: is my influencer agency or my publication studio generating better cost-per-sale?

The agencies can still see their own channel's performance. But they see only their channel. You see everything.

This structure also changes the agency relationship dynamic. When agencies know their performance is being measured against a shared standard, reporting becomes more accurate. Agencies that are actually performing well benefit — their real results are visible and undeniable. Agencies that were hiding behind vanity metrics have nowhere to hide.

The Compounding Benefit

Once you have unified attribution, something interesting starts to happen: you start to see patterns across channels.

Maybe your affiliate conversions spike in the 72 hours after a publication placement. Maybe influencer traffic from Mumbai has a 3x higher LTV than influencer traffic from Delhi. Maybe your Tier 2 affiliate partners are outperforming your metro influencers on cost-per-acquisition.

None of these insights are visible when data lives in silos. All of them become visible when you have a unified view.

The multi-agency problem is solvable. It just requires treating attribution as infrastructure that belongs to the brand, not to any individual agency.
author: MicroKorant Team

title: Affiliate Marketing for Indian Brands: The Ambassador Model
excerpt: Forget global affiliate networks. Indian D2C brands are building ambassador programs where loyal customers become their best sales channel — here's the full playbook.
content:
Affiliate marketing in India is often misunderstood as something that requires signing up for a global network, managing complex integrations, and dealing with international payment rails. Most D2C founders either over-engineer it or skip it entirely.

The brands doing it well are doing something much simpler: building ambassador programs where their own customers become their sales team.

What an Ambassador Program Actually Is

The concept is straightforward. You create a program where anyone a customer, a micro-creator, a college student who loves your product can sign up, get a unique tracking link, share it with their network, and earn commission on every sale they drive.

No complex network membership. No minimum traffic requirements. No 90-day payment delays through a third-party platform. Just a direct relationship between your brand and people who genuinely like what you sell.

Why This Works Particularly Well in India

India's purchasing behavior is significantly influenced by peer recommendations. The "mera dost bola" (my friend told me) factor is real and measurable. Word-of-mouth in India travels through dense social networks families, college alumni groups, WhatsApp communities, apartment society chats.

An ambassador program systematically activates this. You're not creating artificial advocacy. You're giving structure and incentive to advocacy that was already happening informally.

The Mechanics That Matter

A well-run ambassador program has a few non-negotiable components:

Instant approval. If someone applies and has to wait three days for approval, they lose momentum. Auto-approve new ambassadors, give them their link immediately, and let them get started while excitement is high.

Dual attribution. Tracking links capture digital clicks. Discount codes capture the rest — screenshots shared in WhatsApp groups, codes mentioned in voice notes, links that got copied and pasted into other contexts. You need both.

Transparent dashboards. Ambassadors who can see their clicks, conversions, and earnings in real time stay engaged. Opacity kills motivation. Give them a clean view of what they've earned.

Reliable payouts. The fastest way to kill an ambassador program is to be late on payments. Generate monthly payout reports, review them, and pay on time. This is your reputation as a brand to work with.

Commission Structures That Convert

Percentage-based commissions (8–15% of sale value) work well for products where the ambassador is likely to drive considered purchases. A 12% commission on a ₹2,000 supplement order is meaningful — ₹240 per conversion adds up quickly for active ambassadors.

Flat-fee commissions (₹50–₹200 per sale) work better for lower-consideration, impulse-buy products where you want to protect your margin at higher price points.

Hybrid structures a small percentage plus a flat bonus for hitting monthly targets — work well for your most active ambassadors. They create a tiered engagement dynamic that rewards your best performers.

The SEO and Content Multiplier

There's an underrated secondary benefit to ambassador programs: content and SEO surface area.

Every ambassador who writes about your product a WhatsApp message, an Instagram caption, a Twitter thread, a YouTube short — is creating distributed content that mentions your brand. Some of this content will rank in search. Some of it will get reshared. All of it builds ambient brand presence in contexts you didn't orchestrate.

You can't fully control or measure this multiplier effect, but it's real. Brands with active ambassador programs consistently outperform on branded search volume relative to their paid media spend.

Starting Small

You don't need 1,000 ambassadors to see results. Start with 20 of your most loyal customers. Offer them early access to the program, a slightly better commission rate, and personal outreach from your team.

Learn what's working. Refine your commission structure. Build your tracking and payout infrastructure. Then open it up more broadly.

The brands that scale ambassador programs successfully are the ones that started small, nailed the operations, and expanded from a solid base rather than launching publicly before the infrastructure was ready.
author: MicroKorant Team

title: How Indian Brands Should Think About Publication SEO in 2026
excerpt: Editorial placements, podcasts, and review articles aren't just PR they're compounding SEO assets. Here's how to track them, measure them, and use them strategically.
content:
Most Indian brands treat editorial placements as a PR function. You get covered in YourStory or Inc42, someone shares the article on LinkedIn, you get a temporary traffic spike, and then it fades. The agency files it under "brand building" in their monthly report, and everyone moves on.

This is the wrong mental model. Editorial placements are SEO infrastructure that compounds over time. Treated correctly, they're some of the most valuable marketing assets a brand can build.

Why Publications Work Differently Than Ads

When you run a Google Ad or a Meta campaign, the traffic exists while you're paying for it. The moment you stop, it stops. There's no residual value. Every rupee of spend is a one-time event.

A well-placed article in a high-domain-authority publication keeps driving traffic for months or years after it goes live. It might rank for long-tail keywords your own site can't rank for. It passes link equity to your site. It appears in search results for brand queries, adding social proof at the bottom of the funnel.

This compounding dynamic makes publication placements structurally different from other marketing spend. They're assets, not expenses.

The Attribution Problem for Editorial Content

The challenge is that editorial placements are notoriously hard to measure. The traffic paths are indirect. Someone reads an article, doesn't click, searches for your brand later, finds your site, and buys. Which gets credit?

This attribution difficulty is why many brands underinvest in publications. They can't clearly connect the spend to revenue, so they default to channels where the attribution story is cleaner, even if those channels are less valuable in aggregate.

The fix is a proper tracking infrastructure:

  • UTM-tagged redirect links in every article, so direct click traffic is captured
  • Lift analysis on branded search volume in the weeks after a major placement
  • Traffic decay curves showing how each article's click rate holds over time
  • CPC comparisons against paid channels, using the same value-per-click assumptions

The Podcast and Video Layer

Podcasts and YouTube reviews are an undervalued publication category. They don't generate as many direct clicks, but they create deep credibility for a niche audience.

Someone who heard your brand mentioned for 20 minutes on a podcast they trust is a very different prospect than someone who saw a 15-second ad. The consideration depth is incomparable.

Track these placements separately. Measure the traffic bump in the 7 days after each episode drops. Look at whether podcast-driven visitors have higher conversion rates or LTV than other acquisition channels. In our data, they often do.

Building a Publication Strategy

An effective publication strategy has three tiers:

Tier 1 Flagship placements. YourStory, Inc42, Economic Times Retail, Mint. These are high-cost, high-credibility placements that you execute 3–5 times per year. They anchor your brand's legitimacy and generate meaningful link equity.

Tier 2 — Category-specific publications. Industry newsletters, vertical blogs, niche magazines. These are lower cost with higher audience relevance. A placement in a beauty industry newsletter reaches exactly the right buyer persona even if the domain authority is lower.

Tier 3 Creator editorial. YouTube reviews, podcast mentions, long-form creator writeups. These are earned or lightly sponsored, and they generate social proof that ages well.

A brand running all three tiers with proper attribution tracking will, over 18–24 months, build a content ecosystem that drives compounding organic traffic at falling effective CPCs. The early investment front-loads the cost. The back half is essentially free traffic.

What to Track

For every editorial placement, you should be tracking: total clicks from the article, new users vs returning, conversion rate of article-referred traffic, cost per click against your campaign spend, and the article's click rate over time (does it hold, grow, or decay?).

With this data, you can rank your publication partners by ROI exactly as you'd rank influencers or affiliates. The PR function stops being a black box and becomes an optimizable channel like any other.
author: MicroKorant Team

title: Micro-Influencers vs Macro: The Indian D2C Data Story
excerpt: 10 micro-influencers at ₹15,000 each vs one macro at ₹1.5 lakh. We break down what the actual conversion data says — and when each model makes sense.
content:
The micro vs macro influencer debate has been running since the term "micro-influencer" was coined. Everyone has an opinion. Most opinions are not backed by actual conversion data.

Let's look at what the numbers actually say for Indian D2C brands, and more importantly, when each model makes strategic sense.

Defining the Categories

For the Indian market, the relevant segmentation looks like this: nano influencers (under 10K followers), micro influencers (10K–100K), mid-tier (100K–500K), macro (500K–2M), and celebrity/mega (2M+). The interesting strategic decisions typically happen in the micro-to-macro range, where budgets are meaningful but not unlimited.

What the Engagement Numbers Show

Engagement rate consistently decreases as follower count increases. This is not controversial — it's well-documented. A micro-influencer with 25,000 followers in a tight niche might see 6–9% engagement. A macro with 1.2 million followers might see 1.5–2.5%.

But engagement rate is not conversion rate. And this is where the analysis gets interesting.

The Conversion Rate Story

For Indian D2C brands, micro-influencers typically show higher conversion rates on direct click traffic. The reasons are structural: tighter audience affinity, higher perceived authenticity, more personal parasocial relationships with followers.

When someone with 30,000 followers recommends a product, their followers feel like they know this person. The recommendation carries friend-like weight. When a creator with 2 million followers posts a sponsored story, it reads as advertising, even if it's disclosed as a partnership.

The typical conversion delta we see: micro-influencer traffic converts at 3–6%, macro-influencer traffic converts at 1–3%. That's a meaningful difference when you're paying by the post.

But CPM and Reach Have Their Own Logic

Macro influencers aren't wrong for every situation. There are specific scenarios where they make clear strategic sense:

Product launch brand awareness. If you're entering a category for the first time and need broad reach quickly, a macro placement buys you fast surface area. Conversion efficiency matters less when the goal is category entry and brand recognition.

Credibility anchoring. A single feature by a tier-1 creator can anchor your brand's legitimacy in ways that 50 micro placements can't. "As seen with [creator name]" is social proof currency that has real value.

City-specific targeting. Some macro influencers have genuinely concentrated audiences in specific metros. If you're launching in Mumbai and there's a macro creator whose audience is 65% Mumbai, that's a different calculation than a creator whose audience is diffusely national.

The Diversification Case

The most robust influencer strategy for most Indian D2C brands isn't micro OR macro — it's a portfolio approach with clear roles assigned to each tier.

Use 1–2 mid-tier or macro placements per quarter for brand building and category awareness. Use 8–15 micro placements per month for conversion-optimized performance campaigns. Track everything on the same attribution infrastructure so you can actually compare cost-per-sale across tiers.

The brands that get stuck in dogmatic micro-only or macro-only strategies miss this. Micro-only brands achieve great conversion efficiency but grow slowly on brand metrics. Macro-only brands get reach but bleed on cost-per-acquisition.

The Niche Premium

One thing the follower count frame misses entirely is niche depth. A creator with 18,000 followers who's specifically known for Ayurvedic skincare has a different value profile than a lifestyle creator with 250,000 followers who posts about everything.

For category-specific brands — specialized nutrition, niche fashion, specific hobby verticals — these deep-niche micro creators often dramatically outperform their follower count suggests. Their audience has already self-selected into high purchase intent. You're not hoping your message reaches the right person; you're placing it in front of an audience that's already shopping in your category.

Track creator performance at the individual level, not the tier level. You'll find that your top-performing micro creators will surprise you and your worst-performing macro placements will confirm your suspicions.
author: MicroKorant Team
